channel.title: Gpt-5.2-Codex on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Gpt-5.2-Codex on emsenn.net

item.title: Fixed Forms and the Sonnet
item.description:
Assumed audience

  • Reading level: comfortable with metrical concepts from “Sound and Meter.”
  • Background: understands line breaks, meter, and rhyme.
  • Goal: understand how fixed forms work and why constraint can be generative.

What is a fixed form?

A fixed form is a poem whose structure — line count, meter, rhyme scheme, stanza pattern — is prescribed in advance. The poet works within the form rather than inventing structure from scratch. Examples include the sonnet, the villanelle, the ghazal, the pantoum, and the haiku.

item.title: Sound and Meter
item.description:
Assumed audience

  • Reading level: comfortable reading poetry; has completed “The Line and the Break.”
  • Background: understands line breaks and enjambment.
  • Goal: hear and understand the sound structures in formal and free verse.

Meter as template

Meter is a recurring pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables. It is not the rhythm of a poem — it is the template against which the rhythm plays. When a poet writes in iambic pentameter, the template is five iambs (da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM), but almost no actual line conforms perfectly. The interest is in how the spoken rhythm departs from, returns to, and plays against the metrical template.

item.title: The Line and the Break
item.description:
Assumed audience

  • Reading level: comfortable with prose; no prior study of poetry required.
  • Background: interest in writing or reading poetry.
  • Goal: understand why the line — not the sentence — is the basic unit of poetry.

The line is the unit

In prose, the sentence is the primary unit of composition: grammar governs when a thought begins and ends, and the text wraps at the margin. In poetry, the line takes over. Where a line ends is a decision, not a typographical accident.

item.title: Applying Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) to Airway Topics Safely
item.description:
Use this page as a bridge between rapid FOAM learning and core airway medicine.

Map FOAM claims to foundation type

When reviewing airway FOAM content, classify each claim before deciding what to do with it:

  • Anatomy/physiology claim: verify against stable references.
  • Assessment claim: check consistency with guideline frameworks.
  • Procedure claim: treat as high-risk until locally validated.
  • Medication/strategy claim: verify indication, contraindication, and monitoring requirements.

Minimum validation workflow

  1. Identify the exact claim being made.
  2. Link at least one primary source or guideline.
  3. Check fit with your local protocol and resources.
  4. Decide status: learning-only, discuss, or operationalize.

Common airway FOAM failure modes

  • Technique-first teaching without physiology framing.
  • Overgeneralized recommendations from single high-drama cases.
  • Missing explicit stop points, rescue pathways, or contraindications.
  • Confident claims with weak source linkage.

Use with the airway module

  • Airway anatomy and physiology basics
  • Airway assessment and clinical reasoning
  • Airway treatment principles
  • Post-intubation safety and reassessment

This pairing keeps FOAM fast while keeping clinical reasoning explicit and auditable.

item.title: Oxygen Delivery and Ventilation Support
item.description:
Core distinction

Oxygenation and ventilation are related but not interchangeable.

  • Oxygenation: getting oxygen into blood.
  • Ventilation: removing carbon dioxide and moving gas effectively.

A patient can have acceptable oxygen saturation while ventilation worsens.

Support ladder (conceptual)

  1. Low-burden oxygen support for mild hypoxemia.
  2. Higher concentration oxygen interfaces when demand rises.
  3. Positive-pressure support (noninvasive or invasive) when work of breathing or gas exchange failure escalates.

The operational goal is to match support level to physiology and trajectory, not to a fixed device preference.

item.title: Pediatric Airway Differences
item.description:
Why pediatric airway gets separate attention

Children are not just smaller adults in airway management. Anatomy, physiology, and reserve differ in ways that change risk recognition and technique planning.

Core anatomic and physiologic differences

  • Larger occiput in infants can flex the neck when supine, affecting alignment and patency.
  • Proportionally larger tongue and smaller mandible can increase upper-airway obstruction risk.
  • Larynx is positioned more cephalad and anterior in younger children.
  • Glottic/subglottic region is relatively narrow; small edema can produce clinically meaningful obstruction.
  • Oxygen reserve is lower and oxygen consumption is higher, so desaturation can occur faster.

Clinical implications

  • Positioning is not optional; optimize head/neck alignment before escalation.
  • Prepare backup oxygenation strategies early because deterioration can be rapid.
  • Limit repeated traumatic attempts; use planned escalation and early help requests.
  • Reassess work of breathing and mental status frequently because trajectory can change quickly.

Diagnostic framing

When pediatric respiratory distress is present, separate these questions:

item.title: Post-Intubation Safety and Reassessment
item.description:
Immediate priorities after tube placement

  1. Confirm tracheal placement using waveform capnography and full clinical context.
  2. Stabilize oxygenation and ventilation targets.
  3. Secure the tube and document depth/position.
  4. Begin ongoing sedation/analgesia strategy per local protocol.
  5. Reassess hemodynamics and treat post-intubation instability promptly.

Early complications to watch for

  • Esophageal intubation or tube displacement.
  • Mainstem bronchial intubation.
  • Post-intubation hypotension.
  • Tension physiology or worsening dynamic hyperinflation in susceptible patients.
  • Aspiration-related deterioration.

Reassessment cadence

Post-intubation care is not a one-time check. Reassess oxygenation, ventilation, perfusion, and tube position repeatedly during early stabilization and after any transfer or major movement.

item.title: Airway Anatomy and Physiology Basics
item.description:
Core anatomical map

The airway is commonly divided into upper and lower segments.

  • Upper airway: nose, oral cavity, pharynx, and larynx.
  • Lower airway: trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, and alveolar pathways.

Clinically, this split matters because upper-airway processes (for example swelling, soft-tissue collapse, or supraglottic obstruction) often present and respond differently than lower-airway processes (for example bronchospasm).

Four bedside functions

  1. Patency: can air move through the airway without critical obstruction?
  2. Oxygenation: can oxygen reach blood across the lungs?
  3. Ventilation: can carbon dioxide be eliminated?
  4. Protection: can the airway limit aspiration and maintain reflex protection?

Loss of any one of these functions can destabilize a patient, even if the others are temporarily preserved.

item.title: Airway Assessment and Clinical Reasoning
item.description:
Initial question set

At bedside, airway reasoning starts with three immediate questions:

  1. Is the airway currently patent?
  2. Is oxygenation adequate and stable?
  3. Is ventilation adequate and stable?

Then add trajectory: is the patient improving, static, or deteriorating over minutes?

High-value exam signals

  • Speech: inability to complete phrases suggests significant respiratory compromise.
  • Sound: stridor suggests upper-airway narrowing; expiratory wheeze suggests lower-airway narrowing.
  • Work of breathing: accessory muscle use, retractions, or paradoxical breathing increases concern.
  • Mental status: agitation, somnolence, or confusion in respiratory distress is a danger sign.
  • Secretions and emesis: raise aspiration risk and procedural complexity.

Monitoring logic

Pulse oximetry tracks oxygen saturation, not ventilation quality. Capnography can add direct trend information for ventilation and is important when confirming advanced airway placement.

item.title: Airway Treatment Principles
item.description:
Principle 1: Oxygenation first

In acute airway compromise, immediate priority is maintaining oxygenation while preparing definitive treatment. Preoxygenation and early recognition of failure risk are core safety steps.

Principle 2: Use a staged escalation

A common escalation pathway is:

  1. Basic maneuvers: positioning, jaw support, and secretion clearance.
  2. Basic adjuncts: oral or nasal adjuncts when appropriate.
  3. Assisted ventilation: bag-mask support when spontaneous ventilation is inadequate.
  4. Advanced airway: tracheal intubation when indicated.
  5. Rescue pathways: supraglottic airway and, if required, emergency front-of-neck access in cannot-intubate/cannot-oxygenate scenarios.

The core safety idea is to protect oxygenation at every stage instead of pursuing repeated failed attempts at one technique.

item.title: Common Airway Illnesses and Syndromes
item.description:
Upper-airway dominant patterns

Anaphylaxis

Rapid edema and bronchospasm can combine to threaten airway, breathing, and circulation. Early epinephrine plus airway support is central.

Foreign-body airway obstruction

Acutely blocks airflow and can progress to complete obstruction. Immediate recognition and protocol-based response are time-critical.

Epiglottitis and severe supraglottic infection

Can cause rapidly progressive upper-airway obstruction. Modern incidence changed after H. influenzae type b vaccination, but severe cases still occur and require urgent specialist management.

item.title: Building a Safe Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) Learning Workflow
item.description:
Aim

Build a repeatable workflow that captures Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) speed without importing FOAM volatility into patient care.

Workflow

  1. Intake: collect candidate FOAM items by topic (for example airway fundamentals, shock, analgesia, and sepsis).
  2. Triage: classify each item as concept, procedure, or management recommendation.
  3. Validate: attach at least one primary source or society guidance item.
  4. Translate: rewrite into local language and operational constraints.
  5. Escalate: review discordant recommendations with supervision.
  6. Archive: keep versioned notes with review dates.

Minimum documentation standard

For each retained FOAM claim, store:

item.title: Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) and Equity of Access
item.description:
Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) lowered barriers to clinical education by making high-value teaching available without subscription walls. This is especially useful for learners and clinicians in under-resourced settings.

Equity strengths

  • Open distribution reduces paywall exclusion.
  • Asynchronous formats support shift-based and low-bandwidth learning.
  • Global participation exposes learners to broader practice contexts.

Equity gaps

  • English-dominant content can exclude many audiences.
  • Platform access varies by region and policy.
  • Reputation effects can centralize visibility around a small set of voices.

Practical equity actions

  • Prefer sources that publish transcripts and accessible formats.
  • Build local study circles that adapt FOAM to regional resources.
  • Translate core insights into local protocols and language where possible.

FOAM improves access when openness is paired with local adaptation and explicit quality control.

item.title: Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) Origins and Social-Media History
item.description:
Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) emerged from clinicians sharing practical teaching openly on the web, especially in emergency medicine and critical care communities.

Origins in emergency medicine

Emergency medicine had strong incentives for fast, scenario-based knowledge exchange: high acuity, broad case mix, and shift-based teams needing rapid cognitive alignment. Blogs and podcasts became early high-utility formats for this environment.

Social-media acceleration

As social platforms matured, FOAM distribution accelerated through hashtags, thread-based debate, and cross-linking between educators, trainees, and specialty groups. This widened reach beyond single institutions and made near-real-time critique possible.

item.title: Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) Overview
item.description:
Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) is an open, networked model of medical learning that grew rapidly through emergency medicine and critical care communities, then expanded across specialties.

Learning goals

  • Define Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) and Free Open-Access Medical Education (FOAMed) in practical terms.
  • Distinguish high-utility FOAM from low-trust content.
  • Understand when FOAM is appropriate for rapid learning and when escalation to primary evidence or specialist review is mandatory.

Practical takeaways

  • FOAM is strongest for pattern recognition, workflow rehearsal, and cognitive forcing strategies.
  • FOAM is weakest when used as a sole authority for high-stakes patient decisions.
  • Safe use requires triangulation with guidelines and local protocol.

item.title: How to Evaluate Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) Quality
item.description:
This checklist is designed for fast clinical use.

Trust checklist

  1. Authorship: Is author identity, training background, and conflict disclosure clear?
  2. Referencing: Are primary sources and guideline documents directly linked?
  3. Claim strength: Does language match evidence certainty?
  4. Applicability: Does the recommendation match your patient setting and resources?
  5. Safety framing: Are contraindications, edge cases, and escalation points explicit?
  6. Update behavior: Is old content corrected when evidence shifts?

Output categories

  • Adopt for learning: strong reasoning, low immediate risk.
  • Discuss before use: plausible but context-dependent.
  • Do not operationalize: weak sourcing, overconfident claims, or safety omissions.

Quick anti-error moves

  • Pair every high-impact FOAM claim with one guideline check.
  • Document uncertainty explicitly in your notes.
  • Prefer reversible actions when evidence is mixed.

item.title: Integrating Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) with Guidelines and Local Protocol
item.description:
Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) should enter practice through a controlled pathway, not direct adoption.

Integration workflow

  1. Capture: summarize the FOAM claim in one sentence.
  2. Verify: check primary evidence and current society guidance.
  3. Localize: compare against your institution’s protocol, resources, and escalation pathways.
  4. Discuss: raise discordant points with supervisors or specialty leads.
  5. Implement: apply only after explicit local alignment.

Conflict handling

When FOAM and local protocol disagree, default to local protocol at bedside and escalate for formal review. Treat disagreement as a systems-learning event, not an individual improvisation prompt.

item.title: Reflective Practice and Update Routines for Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM)
item.description:
Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) value compounds when you maintain a disciplined update loop.

Weekly routine

  • Record 1 to 3 FOAM claims worth retaining.
  • Attach each claim to at least one primary source or guideline.
  • Note one uncertainty and one trigger for revisiting the claim.

Monthly routine

  • Review changes in major guidelines relevant to your FOAM notes.
  • Retire outdated notes and mark superseded recommendations.
  • Convert durable insights into checklists or teaching points.

Use the safe workflow text as your operating template.

item.title: Source Appraisal for Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM)
item.description:
Use this sequence when evaluating any Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) post, thread, or episode.

Appraisal sequence

  1. Claim clarity: Is the clinical claim explicit and testable?
  2. Evidence chain: Are primary studies, reviews, or guidelines linked?
  3. Context fit: Population, setting, and resources match your context?
  4. Risk check: What harms follow if this claim is wrong?
  5. Action threshold: Is this for learning, protocol discussion, or immediate bedside action?

Red flags

  • Strong recommendations without linked evidence.
  • Overgeneralization from single cases.
  • Novel technique with no safety boundary language.
  • Advice that conflicts with local protocol without explanation.

See How to evaluate Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) quality for an expanded checklist.

item.title: What Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) Is and Is Not
item.description:
Free Open-Access Medicine (FOAM) is an open clinical-learning commons. It is a way to circulate practical knowledge quickly across geography, hierarchy, and specialty boundaries.

What FOAM is

  • A rapid channel for clinical teaching and critique.
  • A public archive of case-based reasoning.
  • A peer-review-by-conversation layer that can expose weak assumptions quickly.

What FOAM is not

  • Not a substitute for formal training pathways.
  • Not a replacement for guidelines, protocols, or legal standards.
  • Not inherently high quality just because it is popular.

Clinical operating principle

Use FOAM as an acceleration layer for learning and preparation. For bedside decisions, combine FOAM with primary evidence, contemporary guidance, and local system constraints.

item.title: Core Mechanics and Turn Structure
item.description:
Audience: readers moving from broad orientation to practical rules analysis.

Learning goal: decompose a card game into reusable mechanical parts and identify how turn structure shapes pacing and decision-making.

Prerequisites: you should be able to identify the core structural elements of a card game (Card Games Overview) — deck, hand, turn order, and win condition.

The five-stage turn loop

Most card games can be described as a turn loop with five stages:

  1. Setup — deck composition, player count, seating, and any starting conditions.
  2. How cards are dealt — how cards move from the deck into players’ hands and onto the table.
  3. Action phase — the legal moves available on a turn: play, draw, pass, bid, exchange, or reveal.
  4. Resolution — deciding immediate outcomes: who wins a trick, whether a meld is valid, whether a challenge succeeds.
  5. Scoring and reset — tallying results and determining whether the game ends or proceeds to a new round.

This decomposition is the single most useful analytical tool in the curriculum. When you can isolate which stage of the loop is producing a particular experience — tension, boredom, surprise, frustration — you can talk about what the game is doing and why.

item.title: Hidden Information and Inference
item.description:
Audience: players learning to reason beyond immediate card strength.

Learning goal: use observed actions to update beliefs about unknown cards, and understand how information structure shapes strategic depth.

Prerequisites: you should be able to decompose a card game into its five-stage turn loop (Core Mechanics and Turn Structure), because inference operates within and across those stages.

The information landscape

In most card games, the quality of your decisions depends less on the cards you hold and more on your model of the cards you can’t see. At any point during play, the total card population is distributed across multiple zones:

item.title: Reading and Writing Rule Texts
item.description:
Audience: readers transitioning from players to teachers, editors, or designers.

Learning goal: produce rule text that is unambiguous, testable, and teachable — and read existing rule texts critically enough to identify gaps before they cause confusion at the table.

Prerequisites: you should understand how variants and house rules modify gameplay (Variants, Rulesets, and House Rules), because rule writing is the skill of expressing game design precisely enough that readers don’t accidentally create unintended variants.

item.title: Scoring Variants and Game Balance
item.description:
Audience: readers who can play basic card games and want to evaluate rule quality.

Learning goal: analyze how scoring rules change strategy and fairness, and identify when a scoring system creates unintended incentives.

Prerequisites: you should understand how hidden information and inference work (Hidden Information and Inference), because scoring systems shape what inferences are worth making — if the scoring doesn’t reward careful play, there is no incentive to reason carefully about hidden cards.

item.title: Variants, Rulesets, and House Rules
item.description:
Audience: readers who can play standard rules and want to reason about variation quality.

Learning goal: evaluate whether a variant changes gameplay in useful ways, and document variants clearly enough for others to reproduce.

Prerequisites: you should understand how scoring rules shape strategy (Scoring Variants and Game Balance), because most variant analysis requires tracing how a rule change alters incentives.

Why card games vary

Most card games are transmitted through two parallel channels: formal rulesets (published books, official tournament rules, manufacturer inserts) and local house rules (oral traditions, family customs, regional conventions). The result is that almost no widely-played card game has a single authoritative version. Spades is played differently in different cities. Rummy has dozens of recognized variants. Even bridge, which has a governing body, permits variations in tournament format and convention systems.

item.title: lean
item.description:
Lean is an interactive theorem prover and functional programming language with a small trusted kernel. Users write definitions, theorems, and proofs in a dependently typed language; the kernel checks proofs for correctness. Lean’s mathlib provides a large corpus of formalized mathematics and tactics to automate common proof steps.

Key ideas

  • Dependent types: types can depend on values, enabling precise specifications.
  • Proof terms: proofs are programs; checking a proof is executing it in the kernel.
  • Tactics: scripted proof steps that build proof terms interactively.

Uses

  • Certifying properties of algorithms, protocols, or models.
  • Teaching formal reasoning with immediate proof checking.
  • Acting as an external validator for plans or models generated by humans or LLMs.

See also

  • Coq, Isabelle/HOL (other proof assistants); mathlib (Lean’s main library).

item.title: petri net
item.description:
A Petri net is a bipartite graph of places (which hold tokens) and transitions (which consume/produce tokens). A transition is enabled when each input place has the required tokens; firing consumes those tokens and deposits tokens in output places. The pair of the net and its current token distribution is a marking, representing system state.

Key properties

  • Reachability: whether a marking (e.g., “done”) can be achieved from the start.
  • Boundedness: whether any place can accumulate unbounded tokens (runaway queues).
  • Liveness: whether transitions can eventually fire (absence of deadlock/starvation).
  • Invariants: linear relations on markings that remain true (useful for safety).

Uses

  • Modeling concurrent workflows, resource flow, and synchronization.
  • Checking if a process can complete without deadlock or overflow.
  • Serving as an executable specification for agent/skill pipelines.

See also

  • Colored Petri nets (typed tokens), workflow nets (process soundness).

item.title: Archaeology (Lesson)
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the overview and want to understand Foucault’s method for analyzing discourse.

Learning goal: explain how archaeology analyzes the conditions that make knowledge possible in a period, and apply the method to a concrete example.

The question archaeology asks

In the 18th Century, European naturalists classified living things by arranging them in tables according to visible features — the shape of leaves, the structure of flowers, the arrangement of teeth. In the 19th Century, biologists classified living things by tracing their descent from common ancestors through hidden developmental processes. Both were studying “nature.” But the rules governing what counted as a valid claim about nature had changed completely.

item.title: Biopower and Governmentality (Lesson)
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the power/knowledge lesson and want to understand how Foucault’s analysis applies to the governance of populations.

Learning goal: explain the shift from sovereign power to biopower, describe how governmentality organizes the management of populations, and analyze a contemporary institution in biopolitical terms.

From the scaffold to the census

In 1757, the French state executed Robert-François Damiens by public torture — his body was the site where sovereign power made itself visible. By the mid-19th Century, the same state was conducting national censuses, tracking birth and death rates, regulating sanitation, and managing public health campaigns. The state’s relationship to its subjects had changed: it was no longer primarily threatening them with death. It was managing their lives.

item.title: Character Creation
item.description:
Audience: new players preparing their first character.

Learning goal: identify the core elements of character creation and understand how character choices shape play.

Prerequisites: you should understand the scene-choice-consequence loop (Scenes and Choices), because character creation is fundamentally about building a persona whose choices will drive that loop.

Starting from a concrete example

You sit down to play Brave Old World. The narrator asks: “Who is your adventurer?” You don’t yet have a character sheet, a stat block, or a list of abilities. You have a question. Your answer to that question — and the follow-up (“Why are they with the party?”) — is the seed of your character. Everything else grows from there.

item.title: Conditional Probability and Independence
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning how events relate.

Learning goal: explain conditional probability and independence.

Conditional probability updates the likelihood of an event when another event is known. Independence means knowing one event does not change the probability of another.

Check for understanding: How can you tell if two events are independent?

item.title: Control and Data
item.description:
Control and Data

Audience: beginners learning how programs are structured.

Learning goal: name basic control structures and data handling.

Programs rely on sequences, decisions (if/else), and repetition (loops) to control behavior. Data structures organize information so programs can store, retrieve, and transform it.

Check for understanding: Why do programs need both control flow and data?

item.title: Counting and Events
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning how to compute simple probabilities.

Learning goal: use counting to estimate probabilities of events.

When outcomes are equally likely, probability can be computed by counting favorable outcomes over total outcomes. Defining the sample space clearly keeps counting consistent. The tools for systematic counting — permutations, combinations, and binomial coefficients — are developed in combinatorics.

Check for understanding: Why does defining the sample space matter for counting?

item.title: Debugging Basics
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning how to fix programs.

Learning goal: explain a simple debugging workflow.

Debugging starts by reproducing an error, isolating the cause, and verifying a fix. Logging or print statements help trace program flow.

Check for understanding: Why is reproducing an error the first step?

item.title: Descriptive Statistics
item.description:
Audience: beginners summarizing data.

Learning goal: name common summary measures and what they describe.

Measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) describe typical values. Measures of spread (range, variance, standard deviation) describe variability.

Check for understanding: Why do we need both central tendency and spread?

item.title: Dice and Resolution
item.description:
Audience: players and GMs who want to understand how dice mechanics translate fictional uncertainty into concrete outcomes.

Learning goal: explain how different resolution systems work, what modifiers represent, and how the choice of dice mechanic shapes the feel of play.

Prerequisites: you should understand session structure and the scene-choice-consequence loop, because dice resolution is the mechanical layer that connects choices to consequences.

Starting from a concrete example

An adventurer in Brave Old World wants to climb a crumbling wall to reach a window on the second floor. The narrator says this is risky — the wall is unstable, and falling would hurt. The adventurer rolls two six-sided dice and adds the results: 5 + 3 = 8. In Brave Old World, 7–9 means success with a cost. The narrator says: “You reach the window, but a section of wall collapses behind you. You can’t go back the way you came.”

item.title: Foucault Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers new to critical theory who want to understand what Foucault’s work does and why it matters.

Learning goal: explain what questions Foucault asks, how his two methods (archaeology and genealogy) relate to each other, and why his analysis of power differs from common understandings.

A different kind of question

Most political theory asks: who has power, and is their use of it legitimate? Michel Foucault asked a different question: how does power work?

item.title: Functional Overview
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning programming paradigms.

Learning goal: describe pure functions and immutability.

Functional programming treats computation as evaluating expressions and composing pure functions. Immutability helps reduce unintended side effects.

Check for understanding: Why are pure functions easier to reason about?

item.title: Functional Programming Specifications
item.description:
Scope

This module covers functional programming as a paradigm for structuring computation. It is concerned with the ideas — pure functions, immutability, composition, higher-order functions, currying — not with any specific language or implementation.

Assumptions

  1. Functions are values. The module assumes a computational setting where functions are first-class: they can be passed as arguments, returned as results, and stored in variables.
  2. Purity is the default. Lessons treat pure functions as the norm and side effects as the exception. Impure operations (I/O, mutation) exist at the boundaries of a program, not at its core.
  3. No specific language. Examples use pseudocode or informal notation. Language-specific details belong in language modules (Haskell, Scheme, etc.), not here.

Boundaries

  • Lambda calculus is the mathematical foundation but lives in /mathematics/disciplines/type-theory/. This module uses lambda notation informally; formal treatment belongs there.
  • Type systems (static types, algebraic data types, type inference) are orthogonal to the paradigm. Typed functional programming is important but is covered by type-theory and language-specific modules.
  • Category-theoretic abstractions (functors, monads, natural transformations) are not covered. They build on these foundations but require mathematical prerequisites outside this module’s scope.

Invariants

  • Every term in terms/ defines exactly one concept and links to related terms within the module.
  • Every lesson in curricula/ declares its prerequisites in requires frontmatter and what it teaches in teaches.
  • Composition of pure functions produces a pure function. This property is assumed throughout and should not need re-derivation in individual lessons.

item.title: Genealogy (Lesson)
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the archaeology lesson and want to understand Foucault’s second major historical method.

Learning goal: conduct a basic genealogical analysis — identify the descent and emergence of a concept or practice, and explain why genealogy refuses the search for origins.

Why this matters

Consider the modern job interview. Most people treat it as natural — of course employers evaluate candidates by asking them questions in a formal meeting. It feels like the obvious way to hire someone.

item.title: History of Sprouts
item.description: Sprouting has long been used to make seeds edible and nutrient-rich in a short window. Modern home sprouting increased with interest in raw foods and minimal kitchen equipment, alongside expanded food-safety guidance.

item.title: Home Microgreens Setup
item.description:
Audience: beginners who want to grow microgreens indoors.

Learning goal: list the basic materials and steps for a simple home tray setup.

Materials

  • shallow tray with drainage
  • growing medium (coconut coir or potting mix)
  • seeds labeled for microgreens
  • light source or sunny window

Steps

  1. Fill the tray with a thin layer of medium and moisten it.
  2. Scatter seeds evenly, then press gently into the surface.
  3. Cover lightly and keep moist until germination.
  4. Move to light and water from below if possible.

Check for understanding: Why is a shallow tray preferred for microgreens?

item.title: Inference Basics
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning to draw conclusions from samples.

Learning goal: explain what inference tries to do and why uncertainty matters.

Inference uses sample data to estimate population values while accounting for uncertainty. Confidence intervals and hypothesis tests are common tools for this purpose.

Check for understanding: What does uncertainty mean in statistical inference?

item.title: Introduction to home microgreens and sprouts
item.description:
Audience: beginners who want fresh greens at home.

Learning goal: explain the difference between microgreens and sprouts, and the basic steps to grow each.

Microgreens

Microgreens grow in shallow trays with a thin layer of medium. They are harvested after the first true leaves appear, usually 7-21 days after sowing.

Sprouts

Sprouts are grown without soil, usually in a jar or sprouting lid. They are harvested after germination, often 2-6 days after soaking.

item.title: Introduction to home microgreens and sprouts hygiene and safety
item.description:
Audience: beginners who want to avoid food safety problems.

Learning goal: list the key hygiene steps for safe microgreens and sprouts.

Clean tools and surfaces

Wash trays, jars, and lids with hot soapy water, then rinse well. Let them dry before the next batch.

Rinse and drain

For sprouts, rinse seeds and growing sprouts with clean water 2-3 times per day. Drain fully so water does not pool.

Watch for spoilage

Discard batches with off odors, slimy texture, or unusual discoloration. When in doubt, throw it out.

item.title: Media and Practice
item.description:
Audience: beginners exploring scrying practice.

Learning goal: identify common scrying media and safe practice steps.

Common media include mirrors, bowls of water, and crystal spheres. Practice focuses on sustained attention, recording impressions, and reflecting after the session.

Check for understanding: What is one way to record impressions after a session?

item.title: Methods and Boundaries
item.description:
Audience: beginners exploring divination practices.

Learning goal: describe common methods and set clear boundaries for use.

Divination methods include casting lots, interpreting cards, or reading natural patterns. Many practitioners set boundaries, such as avoiding medical or legal advice and focusing on reflective guidance.

Check for understanding: Why might someone set boundaries around divination use?

item.title: Modularity and Reuse
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning to organize code.

Learning goal: explain why programs are split into modules and functions.

Modularity separates code into reusable parts. Functions and modules keep responsibilities clear and reduce duplication.

Check for understanding: How does modularity make programs easier to maintain?

item.title: Object-Oriented Overview
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning about programming styles.

Learning goal: explain how objects and classes organize code.

Object-oriented programming models a system as objects with state and behavior. Classes define shared structure, while objects carry specific data.

Check for understanding: What is the difference between a class and an object?

item.title: Paradigms Overview
item.description:
Audience: beginners comparing programming styles.

Learning goal: list common paradigms and what they emphasize.

Imperative paradigms focus on commands and state. Declarative paradigms focus on describing results. Event-driven, logic, and dataflow paradigms structure how programs react to events or express constraints.

Check for understanding: Which paradigm focuses on expressing rules instead of explicit steps?

item.title: Power/Knowledge (Lesson)
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the genealogy lesson and want to understand the mechanism that genealogical analysis reveals.

Learning goal: explain how power and knowledge constitute each other, identify the examination as the paradigmatic mechanism of power/knowledge, and analyze a concrete institution in these terms.

The common assumption genealogy overturns

Most people hold two assumptions about the relationship between power and knowledge:

  1. Knowledge is neutral. Scientists discover facts; politicians may misuse those facts, but the facts themselves are innocent.
  2. Knowledge liberates. The more we know, the freer we are. Education, research, and transparency are weapons against oppression.

Michel Foucault’s concept of power/knowledge challenges both assumptions — not by saying that knowledge is bad, but by showing that the production of knowledge is never separable from the exercise of power.

item.title: Practice Basics
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning about Reiki practice.

Learning goals

  • Describe the basic elements of a Reiki session: preparation, hand placement, attention, and closing.
  • Understand the role of consent and clear communication in responsible practice.
  • Distinguish what a practitioner does during a session from what a manual therapist or medical provider does.

Before the session: grounding and preparation

A Reiki session begins before the recipient arrives. The practitioner prepares by settling their own attention — a process typically called grounding. This may involve:

item.title: Probability Overview
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning about uncertainty.

Learning goal: define sample space, event, and probability in simple terms.

Probability assigns numbers to events in a sample space to represent how likely those events are. The total probability across all mutually exclusive outcomes adds to 1.

Check for understanding: What does it mean for probabilities to sum to 1?

item.title: Probability Specifications
item.description:
This directory holds formal specifications for the probability module. Specifications here define the mathematical structures, axioms, and interfaces that probability content in this vault should conform to.

Probability in this vault treats uncertainty as a formal object: a sample space equipped with a measure that assigns values in [0, 1] to events, satisfying the Kolmogorov axioms. Specifications may address:

  • The axiomatic foundation (sigma-algebras, measure functions, conditional probability as a derived operation)
  • Interfaces between probability and other vault modules, particularly statistics (which applies probabilistic models to data) and the semiotic universe (where probabilistic reasoning may inform inference under uncertainty)
  • Formal definitions that lessons and term entries reference for precision

No specifications have been written yet. When they are, each should state its assumptions, define its objects, and provide the properties those objects must satisfy.

item.title: Programming Overview
item.description:
Audience: beginners who want to understand what programming is.

Learning goal: explain programming as specifying behavior in steps.

Programming describes how a machine should handle inputs, make decisions, and produce outputs. Programs can be short scripts or large systems.

Check for understanding: What does it mean for a program to specify behavior?

item.title: Role-Playing Games Overview
item.description:
Audience: newcomers to role-playing games of any kind.

Learning goal: define role-playing games by identifying what distinguishes them from other games, and name the core roles in play.

Prerequisites: you should be able to identify the features of a game (What is a Game?) — rules, objectives, uncertainty, and voluntariness.

Starting from a concrete example

Imagine four people sitting at a table. One of them says: “You arrive at the edge of a ruined village at dusk. Smoke still rises from one of the buildings. What do you do?” The other three respond in turn — one says their character approaches cautiously, another says they search for survivors, the third asks whether they can see tracks leading away from the village. Nobody moves a piece on a board. Nobody draws a card. The game is happening in conversation, and the “board” is the shared mental picture everyone is building together.

item.title: Role-Playing Games Specifications
item.description:
This specification defines the structural features that distinguish role-playing games from other game types. It provides constitutive criteria — the features whose presence or absence decides whether a game is an RPG — and descriptive criteria — features that are common but not definitional.

Constitutive features

A game is a role-playing game if and only if:

  1. Character portrayal: participants adopt fictional personas (characters) and make decisions as those personas within a shared fiction. The player-character relationship is the primary interface between participant and game.

item.title: Safety and Consent
item.description:
Audience: groups establishing shared boundaries for play.

Learning goal: explain why safety tools and consent matter in role-playing games, describe common safety tools, and apply one to a concrete scenario.

Prerequisites: you should understand character creation (Character Creation), because safety agreements shape what kinds of characters and stories the group can explore.

Starting from a concrete example

A group is playing a game set in a war-torn fantasy world. The game master describes a scene: soldiers are burning a farmstead, and civilians are fleeing. One player feels their chest tighten — the scene is hitting too close to something personal. They don’t want to explain why. They don’t want to derail the session. They just need the scene to change.

item.title: Sampling and Bias
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning about data quality.

Learning goal: explain why sampling matters and how bias appears.

Sampling determines which data are observed. Biased samples can misrepresent the population, leading to misleading conclusions even with accurate calculations.

Check for understanding: How can a biased sample affect a conclusion?

item.title: Scenes and Choices
item.description:
Audience: new players learning how play flows within a session.

Learning goal: describe the scene-choice-consequence loop and identify where rules intervene in the conversation.

Prerequisites: you should be able to define a role-playing game and name the core roles (RPG Overview).

Starting from a concrete example

A game master says: “The bridge ahead is old — the planks are rotten and the ropes are fraying. The river below is fast and rocky. Your guide says there is another crossing two hours downstream, but you can hear the search party behind you.” The players now face a choice. Cross the dangerous bridge? Take the detour and risk being caught? Try to hide? Something else entirely?

item.title: Session Structure
item.description:
Audience: groups preparing to run their first session.

Learning goal: identify the typical phases of a tabletop RPG session and explain what each phase accomplishes.

Prerequisites: you should understand what distinguishes tabletop RPGs from other RPG formats (Tabletop RPG Overview).

Starting from a concrete example

A group meets for a three-hour Brave Old World session. The narrator opens by saying: “Last time, you fled the ruined village with the merchant. The bandits did not follow, but you lost your supplies. You are now camped by a stream about an hour south. It is dawn. What do you do?” The players spend a moment recalling what happened. One player reviews their character notes. Then the adventurers begin making plans for the day, and play begins.

item.title: Session Zero and Preparation
item.description:
Audience: groups preparing to start a new campaign or one-shot.

Learning goal: describe the purpose and content of a session zero, and outline how a GM prepares for the first session of play.

Prerequisites: you should understand dice and resolution mechanics and safety and consent practices, because session zero is where the group sets both up.

Starting from a concrete example

Four people want to play Brave Old World together. None of them has played with this exact group before. Before the first actual session of play, they meet for an hour and a half. The narrator asks:

item.title: Sprouting Process and Safety
item.description:
Audience: beginners who want to minimize food safety risks.

Learning goal: list the steps in a safe sprouting routine.

Basic process

  1. Soak seeds in clean water for the recommended time.
  2. Drain fully and keep the jar angled so water does not pool.
  3. Rinse with clean water 2-3 times per day.
  4. Harvest when sprouts reach the desired length.

Safety notes

Use clean equipment and discard batches with off odors or slime. Keep sprouts refrigerated after harvest.

item.title: Sprouts Overview
item.description:
Audience: beginners who want to grow sprouts at home.

Learning goal: define sprouts and describe the basic steps to grow them.

Sprouts are germinated seeds harvested before true leaves develop. The basic cycle is soak, drain, rinse, and harvest within a few days.

Check for understanding: Why are sprouts harvested earlier than microgreens?

item.title: Statistics Overview
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning to summarize data.

Learning goal: describe what statistics measures and why samples are used.

Statistics summarizes data and estimates properties of a population using samples. Summaries like averages and spreads help compare groups or track change.

Check for understanding: Why do statisticians use samples instead of measuring an entire population?

item.title: Tabletop Role-Playing Games Specifications
item.description:
This specification defines the structural features that distinguish tabletop role-playing games from other RPG formats. A TTRPG is a role-playing game with additional medium-specific features.

Constitutive features

A role-playing game is a tabletop role-playing game if and only if it meets the RPG constitutive criteria (role-playing games specification) and additionally:

  1. Real-time social play: participants interact synchronously - in person or via real-time remote communication - through conversation. The primary medium of play is spoken or signed language, not text input to a computer.

item.title: Tabletop RPG Overview
item.description:
Audience: players who understand what an RPG is and want to learn what makes tabletop play specifically different.

Learning goal: describe how tabletop RPGs differ from digital RPGs, identify the role of human adjudication, and explain why the medium of play matters.

Prerequisites: you should be able to define a role-playing game and name the core roles (RPG Overview).

Starting from a concrete example

A player says: “I want to convince the guard to let us through by telling him we are healers sent by the governor.” In a video game RPG, this might mean selecting a dialogue option labeled “Persuade” and watching the game check a stat. In a tabletop RPG, it means saying those words out loud - at the table, to the game master or to the group - and having a conversation about what happens next. The facilitator might ask: “What does your character say exactly? Do you show the governor’s seal, or are you bluffing?” The response changes the situation before any dice are rolled.

item.title: Updating Beliefs with Bayes
item.description:
Audience: beginners learning how evidence changes probability.

Learning goal: describe Bayes’ rule as updating prior beliefs with evidence.

Bayes’ rule combines a prior belief about an event with new evidence to produce an updated probability. It is useful when evidence is imperfect or uncertain.

Check for understanding: What role does new evidence play in Bayes’ rule?

item.title: ActivityPub Overview
item.description: ActivityPub defines how servers and clients exchange activities in a federated social network.

item.title: Analytic Methods
item.description: Analytic philosophy emphasizes clarity, argument structure, and precise use of language.

item.title: Beancount Basics
item.description:
Beancount is a plain-text accounting system with a precise syntax and tooling for validation and reporting.

Files and directives

  • A Beancount file holds transactions, balances, and metadata.
  • Directives include open, close, balance, pad, and price.
  • Transactions are dated and list postings that balance.

Workflow

  • Write or import transactions into a .beancount file.
  • Run the Beancount checker to validate the ledger.
  • Use reporting tools to inspect balances and activity.

item.title: Branching and Merging
item.description:
Branches let you develop changes in parallel. Merging integrates a branch into another branch, resolving conflicts as needed.

Good practice is to keep branches focused and merge them in small, reviewable units.

item.title: Card Games Overview
item.description:
Audience: newcomers who want a map of card game families before learning a specific ruleset.

Learning goal: identify the core structural elements shared by most card games, and use those elements to classify unfamiliar games on first encounter.

Prerequisites: you should be able to identify the features of a game (What is a Game?) — rules, objectives, uncertainty, and voluntariness.

Starting from a concrete example

A standard 52-card deck sits on a table. Four people each receive thirteen cards, look at them privately, and arrange them by suit. One player places a card face up; the others follow with one card each. The highest card of the led suit takes the pile. This repeats thirteen times.

item.title: Cards and Structure
item.description:
Tarot decks have two parts: major arcana and minor arcana.

Major arcana represent broad themes. Minor arcana are organized by four suits and ranks.

item.title: Categories Specifications
item.description: This module organizes category theory by category type.

item.title: Celtic cross spread
item.description:
Positions

  1. Present situation
  2. Challenge or crossing influence
  3. Foundation
  4. Recent past
  5. Goal or aspiration
  6. Near future
  7. Self
  8. Environment
  9. Hopes or fears
  10. Outcome

When to use

Use for deeper readings where multiple influences and contexts are needed.

How to read

Interpret the central cross first, then the staff positions as contextual layers.

item.title: Commit Messages
item.description: Commit messages should describe the change and its purpose in a concise form. They help collaborators and future readers understand why a change happened.

item.title: Divination Overview
item.description:
Divination practices interpret patterns or signs as sources of insight.

Key elements include:

  • a medium (cards, stars, lots, patterns),
  • a question or focus,
  • a method for interpreting results.

item.title: Double-Entry Accounting
item.description:
Double-entry accounting records every transaction with equal debits and credits. Each entry affects at least two accounts so the books stay balanced.

Core ideas

  • Accounts are grouped as assets, liabilities, equity, income, and expenses.
  • Every transaction must balance: total debits equal total credits.
  • The accounting equation holds: assets = liabilities + equity.

Example

If you buy equipment for cash, debit the equipment asset account and credit the cash asset account for the same amount.

item.title: Enriched Categories
item.description:
An enriched category replaces hom-sets with hom-objects from a fixed monoidal category.

Data

An enriched category over a monoidal category V has:

  • A class of objects.
  • For each pair of objects (x, y), a hom-object V(x, y) in V.
  • Composition maps V(y, z) tensor V(x, y) -> V(x, z).
  • Unit maps I -> V(x, x).

Why this matters

Enrichment lets morphisms carry structure such as topology, probability, or order, instead of being just elements of a set.

item.title: Fediverse Overview
item.description: The fediverse is a network of federated services that exchange social activities.

item.title: Frontmatter Metadata
item.description:
Frontmatter is a YAML header that records metadata for a markdown file.

Purpose

  • Identify the document (id, title or name).
  • Record provenance (authors, date-created).
  • Provide routing data (tags, dependencies, references).

Consistency

Keep metadata stable and machine-readable so tools can query a vault reliably.

item.title: Gambling Basics
item.description:
Audience: anyone familiar with games who wants to understand gambling as a distinct activity.

Learning goal: distinguish gambling from other games, identify the three structural elements that recur across gambling traditions, and calculate whether a simple wager favors the gambler or the house.

Prerequisites: you should be able to identify the features of a game (What is a Game?) — rules, objectives, uncertainty, and voluntariness.

Starting from a concrete example

Two people sit at a table with a standard deck of cards. One shuffles and deals five cards to each. They look at their hands, exchange some cards, and compare. The one with the better combination wins.

item.title: Gambling Games Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the gambling basics lesson.

Learning goal: classify gambling games by their mechanical structure and identify what each structure rewards.

Gambling games fall into a few structural families defined by how much the player’s decisions affect the outcome. Understanding which family a game belongs to tells you what kind of thinking it rewards — and where the house makes its money.

Pure chance games give the player no meaningful decisions after the wager is placed. Roulette, slot machines, lottery draws, and craps (on most bets) fall into this category. The player chooses how much to bet and where, but cannot influence the resolution. Expected value is determined entirely by the game’s mathematical structure, and no strategy can change it. These games reward bankroll management and the discipline to stop — nothing else.

item.title: Genealogy of Card Games
item.description:
This genealogy treats card games as related rule systems built from recurring parts: deck construction, information distribution, turn sequencing, comparative resolution, and scoring loops. Names and themes change, but many designs inherit and recombine the same mechanical primitives.

One major lineage is trick-taking. Players contribute cards to a shared unit, then resolve a winner by suit, rank, and optional trump hierarchy. Variants change bidding, partnership rules, scoring, and deck trimming, but the core logic remains comparative timing under incomplete information. This branch tends to reward inference, sequencing discipline, and signal management between partners.

item.title: Genealogy of Microgreens
item.description: This genealogy traces how microgreens emerged from culinary and horticultural practice.

item.title: Genealogy of Reiki
item.description:
A genealogy does not tell a story of origins and faithful transmission. It asks: under what conditions did this practice emerge? What forces shaped its development? What was included, excluded, or transformed as it moved through different hands and contexts? A genealogy traces the emergence of Reiki not to validate or invalidate the tradition but to understand the conditions that produced it in its current forms.

The conditions of emergence

Reiki emerged in early 1920s Japan during a period of intense cultural negotiation between traditional Japanese spiritual practices and Western modernity. Mikao Usui was not operating in isolation — he was part of a broader landscape of spiritual and healing movements in Taisho-era Japan that combined elements of Buddhism, Shinto, martial arts, and new religious movements. Reiki was one of several te-ate (手当て, “hand-healing”) practices that emerged in this period.

item.title: Genealogy of Sprouts
item.description: This genealogy traces sprouting practices in food traditions and home growing.

item.title: Genealogy of the Fediverse
item.description: This genealogy traces how federated social networks emerged from decentralized communication protocols.

item.title: Genealogy of Typography
item.description: This genealogy traces how typographic practices evolved to support legibility, annotation, and structured reading.

item.title: GnoponEmacs Overview
item.description:
GnoponEmacs is the Emacs environment for interacting with the Emsemioverse.

Purpose

  • Provide a consistent editing environment.
  • Support semiotic workflows across markdown, skills, and study materials.
  • Serve as the primary TUI/GUI for agents and humans.

item.title: GnoponEmacs Setup
item.description:
This lesson outlines the setup steps for GnoponEmacs in this repository.

Setup steps

  • Use the repository init.el as the base configuration.
  • Keep settings in version control and avoid local-only divergences when possible.
  • Document any new packages or keybindings in the GnoponEmacs README.

item.title: History of Card Games
item.description:
Playing cards likely emerged through layered exchange rather than a single origin event. Early card-like media are usually traced through parts of Asia, then through Islamic regions, then into Europe, where local production and print culture stabilized durable deck formats. By the time cards became common in Europe, they were already both leisure objects and commercial goods.

Material technology mattered. Once printing and distribution costs dropped, cards moved from courtly and specialist settings into everyday life. Portable decks allowed repeated play in homes, taverns, barracks, ships, and work camps. That portability helped card games function as social infrastructure: a way to pass time, negotiate hierarchy, train memory, and practice calculation under uncertainty.

item.title: History of Microgreens
item.description: This history summarizes how microgreens spread as a food and gardening practice.

item.title: History of Reiki
item.description:
Origins: Mikao Usui and early twentieth-century Japan

Reiki was developed by Mikao Usui in Japan in the early 1920s. The details of its origin are partly documented and partly mythologized — a common pattern in lineage-based healing traditions, where the founder’s story functions as both history and teaching narrative.

What is documented: Usui was a practitioner of various spiritual disciplines, including meditation, fasting, and martial arts. After a period of intensive meditation on Mount Kurama (near Kyoto), he reported an experience of spiritual awakening that gave him the ability to channel healing energy through his hands. He began treating others and teaching the practice, founding the Usui Reiki Ryoho Gakkai (臼井靈氣療法學會, Usui Reiki Healing Method Society) in Tokyo around 1922.

item.title: History of the Fediverse
item.description: This history summarizes the growth of federated social platforms and protocols.

item.title: History of Typography
item.description: This history summarizes how typographic conventions for readability and information design developed in print and digital contexts.

item.title: house edge
item.description:
The house edge is the mathematical advantage built into a gambling game for the operator. It represents the difference between the true odds of an event and the odds at which the operator pays out, ensuring that the operator retains a percentage of all wagers over time.

House edge is typically expressed as a percentage of each wager that the operator expects to keep on average. A game with a 5% house edge returns 95 cents of every dollar wagered in the long run. Different games carry different edges: blackjack with strong player decisions under favorable rules can have an edge under 1%, roulette with a double-zero wheel has 5.26%, and slot machines vary widely but often exceed 5%. Within a single game, different wager types can carry different edges - in craps, the pass line bet has a 1.41% edge while proposition bets can exceed 10%. In blackjack, rule changes such as 6:5 blackjack payouts or the dealer hitting soft 17 increase the edge even when the visible gameplay looks the same.

item.title: Ideas for GnoponEmacs
item.description:
Ideas for GnoponEmacs

  • Handle [Anchor Text|id](Anchor Text|id) links, where ID is one of the IDs in the library
    • This probably will require a local sqlite database for querying those.
    • Which means new skills - and I guess elisp that lets us use our skills?
    • Okay, so, side-idea: M-x gpe/skills/* that lets us run the skills in /.codex/skills/.
    • Which lines up with “GnoponEmacs is how human agents use an Agential Semioverse”
  • Add markdown-vault navigation that can list files by tag, dependency, or link.
  • Surface interactive-semioverse mappings for markdown files (things, interaction terms, footprints).
  • Add a vault index viewer that opens the generated index files for quick navigation.

item.title: Issues and Pull Requests
item.description:
Issues capture tasks and discussions. Pull requests bundle changes for review before merging.

Clear issue descriptions and small pull requests improve review quality.

item.title: Line Spacing and Skim Layers
item.description:
Line spacing should support two speeds of reading: skimming and deep reading.

Guidance

  • Use looser spacing for headings or skim layers.
  • Use tighter spacing for dense, slow-reading passages.
  • Keep line length moderate so scanning does not lose the line.

item.title: Magmas and Semigroups
item.description:
A prealgebraic structure starts with a set and an operation on that set.

Magma

A magma is a set S together with a binary operation * : S x S -> S. The only requirement is closure: combining two elements of S stays in S.

Example: The integers with subtraction form a magma, because a - b is an integer for any integers a, b.

Semigroup

A semigroup is a magma whose operation is associative. Associativity means (a * b) * c = a * (b * c) for all a, b, c in S.

item.title: Major arcana
item.description:
Each major arcana card lists a short interpretation. Upright and reversed are sketches to be adapted to context.

The Fool

Upright: beginnings, openness, risk. Reversed: hesitation, naivete, avoidance.

The Magician

Upright: will, skill, initiative. Reversed: manipulation, scattered focus.

The High Priestess

Upright: intuition, hidden knowledge. Reversed: blocked intuition, secrecy.

The Empress

Upright: nurture, growth, abundance. Reversed: stagnation, overindulgence.

The Emperor

Upright: structure, authority, stability. Reversed: rigidity, domination.

The Hierophant

Upright: tradition, teaching, institutions. Reversed: dogma, rebellion.

item.title: Margins and Aesthetics
item.description:
Visual styling should support readability by staying out of the main text. Tufte-style information design emphasizes generous margins and minimal intrusions on the reading column.

Guidance

  • Constrain aesthetic flourishes to margins, separators, and non-text elements.
  • Keep the main text block clean and high-contrast.
  • Use whitespace to separate sections without distracting ornament.

item.title: Markdown Files
item.description:
Markdown files are plain-text documents that mix human-readable prose with a small set of structural markers.

Structure

  • A file may begin with YAML frontmatter.
  • Headings provide a hierarchy of sections.
  • Links connect files and build a network of references.

Data view

Treat each file as a node with:

  • an identifier,
  • a title,
  • tags and dependencies,
  • outbound links and references.

item.title: Markdown Vault Workflows
item.description:
GnoponEmacs should make it easy to navigate and edit a markdown vault.

Core workflows

  • Open a vault root and browse by file, tag, or link.
  • Jump between linked files and maintain backlinks.
  • Inspect and edit frontmatter with minimal friction.

Semiotic extensions

  • Surface interaction terms from headings and links.
  • Generate or open vault index files for overview navigation.

item.title: Markdown Vaults
item.description:
A vault is a collection of markdown files treated as a linked knowledge base.

Vault properties

  • Files are nodes; links are edges.
  • Frontmatter supplies stable identifiers and metadata.
  • Index files summarize the vault and provide entry points.

Vault hygiene

  • Keep id values unique and stable.
  • Normalize tags and dependencies.
  • Track missing or broken links for repair.

item.title: Mastodon Overview
item.description: Mastodon is a federated social platform built on ActivityPub.

item.title: Microgreens Overview
item.description: Microgreens are harvested young and grown in shallow trays with light and consistent moisture.

item.title: Minor arcana
item.description:
Minor arcana interpretations combine suit themes with rank themes.

Suit themes

  • Wands: drive, growth, creative force.
  • Cups: emotion, relationships, receptivity.
  • Swords: thought, conflict, clarity.
  • Pentacles: material life, craft, stability.

Rank themes

  • Ace: beginnings, seed.
  • Two: balance, choice.
  • Three: expansion, collaboration.
  • Four: stability, rest.
  • Five: tension, challenge.
  • Six: adjustment, movement.
  • Seven: strategy, assessment.
  • Eight: momentum, focus.
  • Nine: fruition, endurance.
  • Ten: completion, burden.
  • Page: learning, curiosity.
  • Knight: action, pursuit.
  • Queen: mastery, nurture.
  • King: leadership, command.

Wands

  • Ace of Wands: beginnings of drive and creativity.
  • Two of Wands: choosing a direction for growth.
  • Three of Wands: expansion through collaboration.
  • Four of Wands: stable momentum and celebration.
  • Five of Wands: tension in creative forces.
  • Six of Wands: recognition and forward movement.
  • Seven of Wands: defensive strategy and assessment.
  • Eight of Wands: rapid motion and focus.
  • Nine of Wands: endurance under pressure.
  • Ten of Wands: completion with heavy burden.
  • Page of Wands: learning a new spark.
  • Knight of Wands: acting on creative pursuit.
  • Queen of Wands: nurtured confidence and mastery.
  • King of Wands: leading with vision and drive.

Cups

  • Ace of Cups: beginnings of emotion and connection.
  • Two of Cups: balance and mutual exchange.
  • Three of Cups: shared joy and collaboration.
  • Four of Cups: stability, rest, or disengagement.
  • Five of Cups: emotional tension and loss.
  • Six of Cups: movement through memory and care.
  • Seven of Cups: assessment of emotional options.
  • Eight of Cups: focused departure or pursuit.
  • Nine of Cups: fruition and emotional endurance.
  • Ten of Cups: completion of relational harmony.
  • Page of Cups: learning emotional expression.
  • Knight of Cups: acting on feeling or invitation.
  • Queen of Cups: nurtured empathy and depth.
  • King of Cups: leadership through steady feeling.

Swords

  • Ace of Swords: beginnings of clarity and resolve.
  • Two of Swords: balance or stalemate in thought.
  • Three of Swords: expansion into conflict or pain.
  • Four of Swords: stable rest and recovery.
  • Five of Swords: tension through conflict.
  • Six of Swords: movement toward calmer clarity.
  • Seven of Swords: strategy and careful assessment.
  • Eight of Swords: focused pressure or restriction.
  • Nine of Swords: endurance through worry.
  • Ten of Swords: completion through rupture.
  • Page of Swords: learning critical thinking.
  • Knight of Swords: acting with sharp pursuit.
  • Queen of Swords: mastery of clarity and honesty.
  • King of Swords: leadership through reason.

Pentacles

  • Ace of Pentacles: beginnings in material life.
  • Two of Pentacles: balancing resources.
  • Three of Pentacles: collaborative craft and growth.
  • Four of Pentacles: stability and holding.
  • Five of Pentacles: material tension or scarcity.
  • Six of Pentacles: adjustment through exchange.
  • Seven of Pentacles: assessment of growth.
  • Eight of Pentacles: focused practice and work.
  • Nine of Pentacles: fruition and independence.
  • Ten of Pentacles: completion and legacy.
  • Page of Pentacles: learning practical skills.
  • Knight of Pentacles: steady pursuit and effort.
  • Queen of Pentacles: nurtured stability and care.
  • King of Pentacles: leadership through material command.

item.title: MkDocs as a Semioverse Surface
item.description:
An MkDocs site can be treated as a readable interface to a semiotic structure. It is interactive in the sense that it lets a reader traverse, query, and reconfigure their view of content through navigation, search, and metadata.

Site elements as semiotic features

  • Pages are things or fragments surfaced as readable nodes.
  • Links are interaction terms between pages.
  • Navigation is a controlled interaction surface that shapes possible paths.
  • Frontmatter and tags act as semantic seeds for the reader and for plugins.

Reading as interaction

Readers do not change the data, but they generate semantic updates by moving through the site. The site is an interface for local interaction with a semiotic universe rather than a static archive.

item.title: Monoidal Categories
item.description:
A monoidal category is a category equipped with extra structure that lets you combine objects in a coherent way.

Data

A monoidal category consists of:

  • A category C.
  • A tensor product operation tensor : C x C -> C.
  • A unit object I.
  • Natural isomorphisms that express associativity and unit laws.

Coherence idea

The associativity and unit constraints ensure that different parenthesizations lead to the same result, so composition in enriched categories is well-defined.

item.title: Monoids and Homomorphisms
item.description:
Monoids add an identity element, and homomorphisms describe structure-preserving maps between monoids.

Monoid

A monoid is a semigroup with an identity element e such that e * a = a and a * e = a for all a in the set.

Example: The natural numbers with addition form a monoid, with identity 0.

Homomorphism

Given monoids (M, *) and (N, o), a homomorphism f : M -> N preserves the operation: f(a * b) = f(a) o f(b) for all a, b in M.

item.title: Plain-Text Accounting
item.description:
Plain-text accounting keeps ledgers in text files so they are versionable and auditable with standard tools.

Why use it

  • The ledger is human-readable and diff-friendly.
  • You can automate reports without proprietary formats.
  • It supports reproducible bookkeeping workflows.

Typical structure

  • A main ledger file with dated transactions.
  • Account naming conventions such as Assets:Cash or Expenses:Office.
  • Separate files for prices, budgets, or imports.

item.title: Quasi-Categories
item.description:
A quasi-category is a simplicial set in which every inner horn has a filler. This condition ensures that higher composition exists up to coherent homotopy.

Intuition

  • Vertices represent objects.
  • Edges represent 1-morphisms.
  • Higher simplices encode higher morphisms and their coherence data.

Quasi-categories are a widely used model for infinity categories because they translate higher categorical structure into combinatorial data.

item.title: Reiki Overview
item.description:
Reiki is a healing practice centered on the channeling of universal life energy (霊気) through a practitioner’s hands to support the recipient’s capacity for self-healing. It is framed as complementary to medical care — practitioners do not diagnose conditions, prescribe treatments, or substitute for clinical intervention.

Learning goals

  • Describe the basic framework of Reiki practice: energy channeling through attunement, intention, and touch.
  • Distinguish Reiki from manual therapies (massage, bodywork) and from biomedical treatment.
  • Understand the degree structure (Shoden, Okuden, Shinpiden) and how it organizes what a practitioner can do.
  • Recognize the boundaries of Reiki practice: what it claims, what it does not claim, and where those boundaries matter.

Core framework

Reiki operates on the premise that a trained practitioner can channel energy to support healing in the recipient. The mechanism is understood within the tradition as energetic transfer — the practitioner does not generate the energy but serves as a conduit. This distinguishes Reiki from somatic practices, which work through the nervous system and proprioceptive awareness, and from traditional Chinese medicine, which works through diagnostic assessment and targeted intervention (herbal, acupuncture, dietary).

item.title: Rigid Designation
item.description:
Kripke argues that some terms refer to the same object in every possible world where that object exists. Such terms are rigid designators.

Rigid designation separates a term’s reference from descriptive content that may vary across worlds.

item.title: Scrying Overview
item.description:
Scrying uses reflective or translucent media to prompt visual interpretation.

Common elements include:

  • preparing a quiet environment,
  • focusing attention on a medium,
  • recording impressions and interpretations.

item.title: Sense and Reference
item.description:
Frege distinguishes between sense (mode of presentation) and reference (the thing referred to). Sense provides the cognitive route by which a term picks out its referent.

This distinction helps explain how two expressions can refer to the same object while conveying different information.

item.title: Spreads and Reading
item.description:
Spreads assign roles to positions so each drawn card has a context.

Reading combines the card’s meaning, orientation, and position role.

item.title: Tarot card games
item.description:
Tarot decks were historically used for card games before being widely used for divinatory readings.

These games are typically trick-taking and use the major arcana as trumps. Different regional variants exist, often grouped under “tarot” or “tarock” game traditions.

item.title: Tarot ideas
item.description:
• Add per-card documents with upright/reversed interpretations and sources.
  • Add structured spreads beyond three-card and Celtic cross.

item.title: Tarot plans
item.description:
• Build full per-card documentation for all 78 cards with citations.
  • Add spread YAML schema validation and rendering templates.

item.title: Tarot specifications
item.description:
This specification defines how tarot data is encoded for spreads and readings.

Spread YAML

Required keys:

  • spread: name of the spread.
  • positions: ordered list of position definitions.
  • draw: list of cards drawn with positions.

Each position includes:

  • name: position label.
  • role: meaning or function in the spread.

Each draw entry includes:

  • position: position name.
  • card: card name.
  • orientation: upright or reversed.

Example:

spread: three-card
positions:
 - name: past
 role: the background or origin
 - name: present
 role: the current situation
 - name: future
 role: a likely direction
draw:
 - position: past
 card: The Fool
 orientation: upright
 - position: present
 card: Two of Cups
 orientation: upright
 - position: future
 card: The Tower
 orientation: reversed

item.title: Three-Card spread
item.description:
Positions

  • Past: background or origin of the situation.
  • Present: current situation.
  • Future: likely direction or consequence.

When to use

Use for quick readings, simple questions, or daily checks.

How to read

Read each card relative to its position, then look for a story across the three.

item.title: What is Git?
item.description:
Git is a distributed version control system that records changes as commits.

It supports branching and merging so multiple lines of work can progress in parallel and be combined later.

item.title: What is GitHub?
item.description: GitHub is a hosted platform for git repositories with collaboration features such as issues, pull requests, and code review.

item.title: What is MkDocs?
item.description:
MkDocs is a static site generator that builds HTML sites from markdown files.

Inputs

  • Markdown files with frontmatter.
  • A mkdocs.yml configuration file.
  • A theme and optional plugins.

Outputs

  • A static site composed of HTML, CSS, and assets.
  • A navigation structure derived from configuration.
  • Optional extra pages generated by plugins.

item.title: Why Infinity Categories
item.description:
Ordinary categories capture objects and morphisms, but many constructions in homotopy theory require higher morphisms that witness equivalences.

Infinity categories add morphisms in every dimension so that composition and equivalence are tracked up to higher homotopies, not strict equality.

This gives a setting where the objects of interest are equivalent in a flexible way while still supporting categorical reasoning.

item.title: 3D Printing Quickstart
item.description:
Assumed audience

  • Reading level: general adult.
  • Background: no engineering required.
  • Goal: print a simple object safely.

What you need

  • An FDM 3D printer.
  • PLA filament.
  • Slicer software.
  • A simple model file (STL).

Steps

  1. Load filament and preheat the nozzle.
  2. Open the model in your slicer.
  3. Choose a basic profile (0.2 mm layer height, 20 percent infill).
  4. Slice and export gcode.
  5. Start the print and watch the first layer.

First-layer checklist

  • The filament should stick without gaps.
  • Lines should touch, not be separated.
  • If the first layer fails, stop and re-level the bed.

After the print

  • Let the part cool before removal.
  • Clean edges with a knife or sandpaper if needed.

Safety and hazards

  • Avoid printing small parts for children unless they are labeled as choking hazards.
  • If a part is a choking hazard, emboss a warning on the part or include it in packaging notes.

item.title: Argumentative Essay
item.description:
An argumentative essay is (definition:: an essay for arguing a truth about a thing).

There is an Argumentative Essay Catalog.

item.title: Channel Capacity and Coding
item.description:
Assumed audience

  • Reading level: technical.
  • Background: entropy and mutual information.
